The evolution of continental crust is a fundamental issue in solid Earth science. Granitoids, as key products of the lower crust, record these processes. The Jiaodong Peninsula hosts three stages of Late Mesozoic granitoids and provides insights into cratonic lower crustal modification. This study integrates geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Hf-O isotopes to constrain these processes. Nb-Ta-Zr-Hf elemental and Hf-O isotopic compositions define a NW-SE trending geochemical boundary (∼121°E). This boundary separates the strongly modified Jiaobei Terrane from the weakly modified Sulu Terrane and aligns with tectonic subdivisions along the Wulian-Yantai fault zone. Three stages of lower crustal modification are identified. Stage I granitoids (166–141 Ma) show variable εHf(t) values (−26.5 to −13.1), δ18O values (4.58–8.93‰), and low Mg# (16–34). These features indicate partial melting of a thickened ancient lower crust during subduction of the Paleo-Pacific Plate. Stage II granitoids (133–121 Ma) display higher εHf(t) values (–23.0 to −11.2), elevated δ18O values (8.09–9.03‰), and Mg# of 26–58. These characteristics indicate melting of a weakly modified lower crust triggered by underplating of enriched mantle materials metasomatized by high-δ18O fluids during slab rollback. Stage III granitoids (120–107 Ma) exhibit high εHf(t) values (–22.3 to −13.9), lower δ18O values (5.34–8.09‰), and higher Mg# (17–64). These data indicate melting of a strongly modified lower crust influenced by enriched mantle materials metasomatized by low-δ18O melts and sediments during continued slab rollback. Integrated petrogenetic, spatiotemporal, and geophysical evidence supports a progressive lower crustal modification model controlled by subduction and slab rollback processes.

item.title: Mid-Cretaceous porphyritic magmatism in the Beartooth Mountains of the northern Laramide foreland and its connection to Frontier Formation sediment dispersal in Bighorn basin, Montana-Wyoming (USA), and Cordilleran tectonism
item.dc:creator: Jacob O. Thacker
Nicholas A. Brailer
Gary S. Michelfelder
Barry J. Shaulis
Rebecca A. VanderLeest
Snir Attia
Eddy Cunningham
item.description: Porphyritic intrusions in the southeast Beartooth Mountains and porphyritic cobbles from the Torchlight conglomerate of the Cenomanian Frontier Formation in Bighorn basin (Montana-Wyoming, USA) bear striking resemblance. We utilize geologic mapping, petrography, geochemistry, and geochronology to characterize rocks from both locations and test whether the Beartooth Mountains area was the provenance for Bighorn basin cobbles. Mapping on Line Creek Plateau in the Beartooth Mountains shows three units with porphyritic phenocrysts consisting dominantly of plagioclase, plagioclase + potassium feldspar, and plagioclase + quartz. Petrography shows embayed quartz, opacitic rims, and zoned plagioclase. Geochemistry characterizes the intrusions as trachydacite to trachyte and trace elements exhibit enrichment in light rare earth elements and slight depletion in heavy rare earth elements relative to primitive mantle. Zircon U-Pb dating shows substantial Archean inheritance and ca. 104−92 Ma dates; some samples display multiple mid-Cretaceous date populations. Bighorn basin porphyritic cobbles exhibit similar modal mineralogy, near-identical geochemistry, and Archean inheritance and mid-Cretaceous geochronology. We suggest the porphyritic cobbles were sourced from basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions now exposed in the Beartooth Mountains, given that geochemistry does not agree with coeval volcanic sources along the Frontier Formation depositional fairway (Idaho batholith) or sedimentary-hosted Beartooth Mountains porphyritic sills intruded into Cambrian shale. Stratigraphic evidence does not support kilometer-scale mid-Cretaceous Beartooth Mountains exhumation to expose basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions, and we therefore present a volcanic hypothesis to bring rocks to the surface. This mid-Cretaceous multimillion-year magmatism is unexplained by tectonic models for the north-central Laramide foreland, and thus we further explore the emplacement processes, spatiotemporal significance, and tectonic implications of these rocks. Published: 2026-06-24T20:49:18+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.562 (consider)

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

Relevance: 0.562 (consider)

Porphyritic intrusions in the southeast Beartooth Mountains and porphyritic cobbles from the Torchlight conglomerate of the Cenomanian Frontier Formation in Bighorn basin (Montana-Wyoming, USA) bear striking resemblance. We utilize geologic mapping, petrography, geochemistry, and geochronology to characterize rocks from both locations and test whether the Beartooth Mountains area was the provenance for Bighorn basin cobbles. Mapping on Line Creek Plateau in the Beartooth Mountains shows three units with porphyritic phenocrysts consisting dominantly of plagioclase, plagioclase + potassium feldspar, and plagioclase + quartz. Petrography shows embayed quartz, opacitic rims, and zoned plagioclase. Geochemistry characterizes the intrusions as trachydacite to trachyte and trace elements exhibit enrichment in light rare earth elements and slight depletion in heavy rare earth elements relative to primitive mantle. Zircon U-Pb dating shows substantial Archean inheritance and ca. 104−92 Ma dates; some samples display multiple mid-Cretaceous date populations. Bighorn basin porphyritic cobbles exhibit similar modal mineralogy, near-identical geochemistry, and Archean inheritance and mid-Cretaceous geochronology. We suggest the porphyritic cobbles were sourced from basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions now exposed in the Beartooth Mountains, given that geochemistry does not agree with coeval volcanic sources along the Frontier Formation depositional fairway (Idaho batholith) or sedimentary-hosted Beartooth Mountains porphyritic sills intruded into Cambrian shale. Stratigraphic evidence does not support kilometer-scale mid-Cretaceous Beartooth Mountains exhumation to expose basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions, and we therefore present a volcanic hypothesis to bring rocks to the surface. This mid-Cretaceous multimillion-year magmatism is unexplained by tectonic models for the north-central Laramide foreland, and thus we further explore the emplacement processes, spatiotemporal significance, and tectonic implications of these rocks.

item.title: Genetic types, mineralization patterns and exploration implications of ore-hosting fractures in porphyry-type deposits
item.dc:creator: Mao-Chun Zhao
Yan-Jun Wang
Hai-Jun Yu
Xi-Lian Chen
Da-Zhao Wang
Jin-Song Yang
Yan Zhang
Cheng-Biao Leng
item.description: Fractures concentrating around shallow granitic plutons are the key deposition site for porphyry-type ores. Early studies have proposed various formation mechanisms for different types of fractures, but these mechanisms are yet to be well integrated with the porphyry ore-forming processes. Here, we present a synthetic study on the formation mechanism and influence factors of fracture network development in/around the ore-causative porphyries during their emplacement, cooling/solidification, and alteration. According to their order of appearance, the fractures in porphyry systems can be divided into three types: (1) intrusion-induced type, (2) magma cooling-related type (i.e., cooling fractures), and (3) magmatic fluid-related hydraulic type (i.e., hydraulic fractures). Intrusion-induced fractures are formed by the compression on wallrocks exerted by magma emplacement, and are mostly distributed in the wallrocks around the pluton apex. Cooling fractures are only developed within the porphyries during magma cooling and solidification. Hydraulic fractures are largely formed by magmatic-hydrothermal pressure-driven cracking. The occurrence, geometry, orientation, and ore grade of the porphyry-type orebodies are all controlled by these three fracture systems, which offers insights for ore prospecting: (1) Intrusion-induced fracture-controlled orebodies are bell-shaped overlying the porphyry bodies, with ore grade gradually dropping away from the porphyries. The veins are relatively straight, and generally comprise a steep- (major) and a shallow- (minor) dipping group. They are few-mm to few-cm wide, and several meters long; (2) Cooling fracture-controlled orebodies are also bell-shaped and commonly developed in the densely-fractured porphyry apex, with the ore grade decreasing away from the porphyry margin toward depth. The veins are generally few-tens of cm long and within a few-mm wide, and are homogeneously distributed with no preferred orientation; (3) Hydraulic fracture-controlled orebodies are generally developed as pipes in the axial part of the porphyries, and can vertically extend over 1000 m. The veins are mostly steep and few-mm to few-cm wide, and several to tens of meters long. Published: 2026-06-24T16:19:40+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.557 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mao-Chun Zhao; Yan-Jun Wang; Hai-Jun Yu; Xi-Lian Chen; Da-Zhao Wang; Jin-Song Yang; Yan Zhang; Cheng-Biao Leng

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107412

Relevance: 0.557 (consider)

Fractures concentrating around shallow granitic plutons are the key deposition site for porphyry-type ores. Early studies have proposed various formation mechanisms for different types of fractures, but these mechanisms are yet to be well integrated with the porphyry ore-forming processes. Here, we present a synthetic study on the formation mechanism and influence factors of fracture network development in/around the ore-causative porphyries during their emplacement, cooling/solidification, and alteration. According to their order of appearance, the fractures in porphyry systems can be divided into three types: (1) intrusion-induced type, (2) magma cooling-related type (i.e., cooling fractures), and (3) magmatic fluid-related hydraulic type (i.e., hydraulic fractures). Intrusion-induced fractures are formed by the compression on wallrocks exerted by magma emplacement, and are mostly distributed in the wallrocks around the pluton apex. Cooling fractures are only developed within the porphyries during magma cooling and solidification. Hydraulic fractures are largely formed by magmatic-hydrothermal pressure-driven cracking. The occurrence, geometry, orientation, and ore grade of the porphyry-type orebodies are all controlled by these three fracture systems, which offers insights for ore prospecting: (1) Intrusion-induced fracture-controlled orebodies are bell-shaped overlying the porphyry bodies, with ore grade gradually dropping away from the porphyries. The veins are relatively straight, and generally comprise a steep- (major) and a shallow- (minor) dipping group. They are few-mm to few-cm wide, and several meters long; (2) Cooling fracture-controlled orebodies are also bell-shaped and commonly developed in the densely-fractured porphyry apex, with the ore grade decreasing away from the porphyry margin toward depth. The veins are generally few-tens of cm long and within a few-mm wide, and are homogeneously distributed with no preferred orientation; (3) Hydraulic fracture-controlled orebodies are generally developed as pipes in the axial part of the porphyries, and can vertically extend over 1000 m. The veins are mostly steep and few-mm to few-cm wide, and several to tens of meters long.

item.title: Characterization of the Shiretokoiozan volcano hydrothermal system in Hokkaido, North Japan: Possible source and mechanism of molten sulfur eruptions
item.dc:creator: Mutsunori Yamamoto
Koki Kashiwaya
Brenda A. Kusumasari
Yohei Tada
Tada-nori Goto
Katsuaki Koike
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research
item.description: Shiretokoiozan volcano in North Japan is known for repeated molten sulfur eruptions. Although the generation of sulfur in the aquifer has been interpreted as the most plausible source of the molten sulfur, the detailed mechanism of its generation is unclear. This study examines the sulfur generation mechanism by estimating the aquifer structure, mixture of groundwater with magmatic water, and water flow system via geochemical water and gas sample analyses for isotope, water, and gas compositions and the reservoir temperature. A mixture of meteoric water with andesitic water and gas in the aquifer in a high self-potential zone is indicated by the water isotope analysis. The average recharge elevation of the hot spring water is 860 m above sea level (masl) according to altitude effects. A schematic flow model indicates that the recharged water flows through the mixing zone at 700 masl and the eruption point at 600 masl and then discharges near the Kamuiwakka hot spring at 330–500 masl. H2S and SO2 were confirmed in the fumarole gas in the high self-potential zone, and a high SO4 2− concentration was measured in the hot spring water. Consequently, the gases and the andesitic water are interpreted as being the main source of sulfur; their mixing with meteoric-sourced water in the aquifer generated sulfur that then erupted downstream through an open fracture from the aquifer at Crater I owing to gravity and the inner pressure. Published: 2026-06-22T23:23:40+00:00 Venue: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research Score: 0.556 (consider)

Shiretokoiozan volcano in North Japan is known for repeated molten sulfur eruptions. Although the generation of sulfur in the aquifer has been interpreted as the most plausible source of the molten sulfur, the detailed mechanism of its generation is unclear. This study examines the sulfur generation mechanism by estimating the aquifer structure, mixture of groundwater with magmatic water, and water flow system via geochemical water and gas sample analyses for isotope, water, and gas compositions and the reservoir temperature. The average recharge elevation of the hot spring water is 860 m above sea level (masl) according to altitude effects. A schematic flow model indicates that the recharged water flows through the mixing zone at 700 masl and the eruption point at 600 masl and then discharges near the Kamuiwakka hot spring at 330–500 masl. H2S and SO2 were confirmed in the fumarole gas in the high self-potential zone, and a high SO4 2− concentration was measured in the hot spring water. Consequently, the gases and the andesitic water are interpreted as being the main source of sulfur; their mixing with meteoric-sourced water in the aquifer generated sulfur that then erupted downstream through an open fracture from the aquifer at Crater I owing to gravity and the inner pressure.

item.title: Progressive mantle melting and crust–mantle–fluid hybridization in fluorite-bearing Sn–Nb ± Mo-rich alkaline granites of the Jos Plateau, central Nigeria
item.dc:creator: Vandi Dlama Kamaunji
Hafizullah Abba Ahmed
Lian-Xun Wang
Ioan V. Sanislav
Musa Bala Girei
Yu-Xiang Zhu
Xin-xin Liang
Xin Chen
Mabrouk Sami
item.description: Alkaline granites of the Jos Plateau Tin Belt (central Nigeria) host Sn-Nb-Mo mineralization. However, their petrogenesis, metal enrichment, and tectono-geodynamic evolution remain poorly constrained. This study integrates whole-rock geochemistry and Sr-Nd isotopes, amphibole-fluorite chemistry, and zircon U-Pb geochronology from the Buji, Jos-Bukuru (JBK), and Kigom (KGM) complexes. Zircon U-Pb ages reveal a north-to-south younging trend from Buji (∼179 Ma) through JBK (∼168 Ma) to KGM (∼166 Ma). Buji barren granites exhibit the most negative εNd(t) (–4.4 to –4.2), least radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7065–0.7066), and highest Neodymium Crustal Index (NCI = 0.31), indicating an enriched mantle source with strong crustal input. JBK Sn–Nb biotite-amphibole granites show intermediate εNd(t) (–3.3 to –3.0), NCI (0.25), and moderately radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7066–0.7173), suggesting an enriched mantle source with moderate crustal input and selective hydrothermal overprint. KGM Mo–Nb arfvedsonite–riebeckite granites exhibit near-chondritic εNd(t) (–1.1 to –0.4), lowest NCI (0.25), and most radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i)r (0.7139–0.7206), reflecting extreme fluid-mediated metasomatism on a mantle-dominated magma. Decoupled fO2 distinguishes oxidized JBK Ca-amphiboles from reduced KGM Na-amphiboles hosting oxidized zircons. Mass balance modeling indicates ∼40–50 vol% feldspar-dominated fractional crystallization coupled with ∼30–40 vol% upper crustal assimilation. JBK granites show Sn-Nb enrichment (4–126 ppm; 37–910 ppm), while KGM granites exhibit high Nb with extreme Zn-Pb-Cu (≤1326, 1054, 845 ppm), consistent with Mo-enriched systems. Fluorite compositions (F = 52–56 wt%) record F-rich reducing fluids that promoted HFSE-REE-Sn-Nb-Mo mobility. A four-stage model explains Sn-Nb-Mo enrichment in JBK and KGM: (I) magmatic enrichment; (II) magmatic-hydrothermal transition; (III) hydrothermal veining and stockworks formation; (IV) supergene placer formation. Transtension triggered adiabatic melting of metasomatized mantle, generating alkaline magmas that ascended along shear zones to form shallowly emplaced (2.6–8.7 km) anorogenic alkaline complexes. Fertile global analogues share this convergence of transtensional shearing, crustal assimilation, and F-rich fluid flux. Published: 2026-06-25T15:14:33+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.551 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vandi Dlama Kamaunji; Hafizullah Abba Ahmed; Lian-Xun Wang; Ioan V. Sanislav; Musa Bala Girei; Yu-Xiang Zhu; Xin-xin Liang; Xin Chen; Mabrouk Sami

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107405

Relevance: 0.551 (consider)

Alkaline granites of the Jos Plateau Tin Belt (central Nigeria) host Sn-Nb-Mo mineralization. However, their petrogenesis, metal enrichment, and tectono-geodynamic evolution remain poorly constrained. This study integrates whole-rock geochemistry and Sr-Nd isotopes, amphibole-fluorite chemistry, and zircon U-Pb geochronology from the Buji, Jos-Bukuru (JBK), and Kigom (KGM) complexes. Zircon U-Pb ages reveal a north-to-south younging trend from Buji (∼179 Ma) through JBK (∼168 Ma) to KGM (∼166 Ma). Buji barren granites exhibit the most negative εNd(t) (–4.4 to –4.2), least radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7065–0.7066), and highest Neodymium Crustal Index (NCI = 0.31), indicating an enriched mantle source with strong crustal input. JBK Sn–Nb biotite-amphibole granites show intermediate εNd(t) (–3.3 to –3.0), NCI (0.25), and moderately radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7066–0.7173), suggesting an enriched mantle source with moderate crustal input and selective hydrothermal overprint. KGM Mo–Nb arfvedsonite–riebeckite granites exhibit near-chondritic εNd(t) (–1.1 to –0.4), lowest NCI (0.25), and most radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i)r (0.7139–0.7206), reflecting extreme fluid-mediated metasomatism on a mantle-dominated magma. Decoupled fO2 distinguishes oxidized JBK Ca-amphiboles from reduced KGM Na-amphiboles hosting oxidized zircons. Mass balance modeling indicates ∼40–50 vol% feldspar-dominated fractional crystallization coupled with ∼30–40 vol% upper crustal assimilation. JBK granites show Sn-Nb enrichment (4–126 ppm; 37–910 ppm), while KGM granites exhibit high Nb with extreme Zn-Pb-Cu (≤1326, 1054, 845 ppm), consistent with Mo-enriched systems. Fluorite compositions (F = 52–56 wt%) record F-rich reducing fluids that promoted HFSE-REE-Sn-Nb-Mo mobility. A four-stage model explains Sn-Nb-Mo enrichment in JBK and KGM: (I) magmatic enrichment; (II) magmatic-hydrothermal transition; (III) hydrothermal veining and stockworks formation; (IV) supergene placer formation. Transtension triggered adiabatic melting of metasomatized mantle, generating alkaline magmas that ascended along shear zones to form shallowly emplaced (2.6–8.7 km) anorogenic alkaline complexes. Fertile global analogues share this convergence of transtensional shearing, crustal assimilation, and F-rich fluid flux.
